summary: The author reflects on his EMT training experience where he discovered that electronic patient care reports (ePCRs), despite being objectively better than paper in almost every way, went unused because of a quarter-second lag on interactions. This seemingly trivial performance issue meant EMTs stuck with error-prone paper forms. At the scale of 30 million PCRs filed annually in the US, even small inefficiencies or error rates translate into lost doctor-hours and potentially lost lives. The post argues that performance isn't just a technical concern but a moral one when software operates at scale.
key_points: A quarter-second lag was enough to make EMTs abandon a superior electronic system entirely and return to error-prone paper forms
At scale, small probabilities become certainties: 0.1% of 30 million PCRs with errors means 30,000 wasted doctor-hours
Software doesn't need to be 'critical' in the traditional sense to have life-or-death consequences at sufficient scale
The diffuse, statistical nature of these harms makes them invisible and difficult to reason about
Performance decisions made during development may have consequences the developers never anticipated or considered
money_quotes: At that scale, low chances kill people. Murdered by statistics.
At that scale, time and life are interchangeable.
It's a factor so grand and diffuse we can't see it in motion. We can't think in those terms.
Paper was slow and annoying and easy to screw up, but at least it wasn't *that*.
Did they say 'premature optimization is bad' and not think about performance until it was too late?
Most of us aren't writing critical software. But this isn't 'critical software', either: nobody will suddenly die if it breaks.
Can a slow CSV library eventually cause a car accident? I don't know.
key_insight: Performance isn't a luxury or premature optimization—at sufficient scale, a quarter-second lag can be the difference between software that saves lives and software that sits unused while people die from preventable errors.
